text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Margaret Yuen
DOB: [redacted] | Age: 63
Diagnosis: Stage IIIB Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, Adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant (T3 N3 M0, AJCC 8th ed.)
Treating Oncologist: [attending name on file]
Visit type: Established patient, oncology follow-up (3-month restaging)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Yuen is a 63-year-old woman with known Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant right lung adenocarcinoma, presenting today for her 3-month restaging visit while on Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Yuen was initially diagnosed approximately 3 months ago with Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0) right upper lobe adenocarcinoma. At that time she presented with a 3-month history of progressive cough, two episodes of mild hemoptysis, and approximately 8 pounds of unintentional weight loss with exertional dyspnea. Staging workup with whole-body PET-CT confirmed a 5.8 x 4.2 cm hypermetabolic right upper lobe mass with right hilar, right paratracheal, and right supraclavicular nodal involvement (N3 disease). Brain MRI was negative for intracranial metastases at the time of diagnosis. CT-guided biopsy confirmed invasive adenocarcinoma with EGFR L858R mutation on exon 21 (cobas PCR, allele frequency 38.2%). PD-L1 TPS was 5% by 22C3. Comprehensive genomic profiling via FoundationOne CDx additionally identified a co-occurring TP53 R248W mutation and low-level MET copy gain (ratio 2.4, not meeting threshold for amplification).

Multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed the case and given her N3 (supraclavicular) nodal involvement, she was deemed a non-surgical candidate. Concurrent chemoradiation was discussed at length; however, the patient declined given concerns about toxicity. Given the presence of a sensitizing EGFR mutation (L858R), the decision was made to initiate osi 80 mg orally once daily as definitive first-line systemic therapy, consistent with evolving evidence supporting EGFR TKI use in unresectable stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC.

She started Tagrisso approximately 3 months ago. Approximately 3 weeks after initiation she developed expected class-effect toxicities: a grade 1 acneiform papulopustular rash across the face (managed with topical clindamycin 1% solution and urea-based moisturizer), grade 1 paronychia of the right index finger, and grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools per day above baseline, managed with dietary modification). No dose reduction was required. She reports that the facial rash has now improved substantially -- she describes it as much less noticeable compared to weeks 3-4 and notes it has not interfered significantly with her daily activities.

Regarding her cancer-related symptoms: the cough that was her primary presenting complaint has improved markedly, and she reports it is now only intermittent and mild, occurring maybe once or twice per day. There have been no further episodes of hemoptysis since starting treatment. Her energy level has improved substantially -- she describes herself as feeling "more like herself" and has been able to take daily walks of 20-30 minutes. She reports that her appetite has improved and she has regained approximately 4 of the 8 pounds she had lost. She denies chest pain, new shortness of breath at rest, or worsening exertional dyspnea compared to her initial presentation.

She denies any new neurological symptoms: no headaches, no visual changes, no focal weakness, no dizziness. She continues to work part-time in a tutoring capacity from home while on leave from her full-time teaching position.

She completed restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast prior to today's visit. Results reviewed below.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports improved energy; weight has stabilized and trending upward (+4 lbs recovered). No night sweats. Low-grade fatigue present but improved compared to baseline at diagnosis.
Pulmonary: Cough markedly improved, now intermittent and mild. No hemoptysis. Mild exertional dyspnea present but improved -- able to walk on level ground without significant limitation. No pleuritic chest pain.
Cardiovascular: No palpitations, no lower extremity edema, no chest pressure.
Gastrointestinal: Mild intermittent loose stools (1-2 times daily, now grade 1 or less), improved from early treatment weeks. No blood in stool, no nausea or vomiting. Appetite improving.
Skin/Integument: Facial papulopustular rash improving; now mild (grade 1 or less). Paronychia of right index finger remains present but not worsening, patient using warm water soaks and topical antibiotic. No other skin toxicities.
Neurological: No headaches, no cognitive changes, no focal neurological symptoms.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain.
Other systems: No dysuria, no new lymphadenopathy noted by patient, no oral mucositis.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

1. Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily -- EGFR TKI, started 3 months ago
2. Topical clindamycin phosphate 1% solution -- applied to face twice daily for acneiform rash
3. Urea 20% cream -- applied to affected skin areas once daily
4. Acetaminophen 500 mg orally as needed for minor discomfort (paronychia)
5. Multivitamin with vitamin D -- daily

Allergies: NKDA (no known drug allergies)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.7 degrees C, SpO2 97% on room air, height 160 cm, weight 56.2 kg (up from 54.0 kg at last visit approximately 6 weeks ago).

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-appearing East Asian woman in no acute distress. Affect is pleasant and engaged. She is dressed neatly and appears well.

HEENT: Scant residual papulopustular lesions across bilateral cheeks and forehead, consistent with healing grade 1 acneiform rash -- much improved from prior examination. No oral mucosal ulcerations. Pupils equal and reactive to light.

Neck/Lymphatics: Previously palpable right supraclavicular lymph node -- today this is no longer clearly palpable. No cervical or left supraclavicular adenopathy. No axillary adenopathy appreciated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No S3 or S4. No jugular venous distension.

Pulmonary: Breath sounds are present bilaterally and improved -- previously noted decreased air entry at the right apex. Today there is still mildly diminished breath sounds over the right upper lobe region, but improved compared to initial examination. No wheezes, crackles, or pleural rub. Good air movement overall.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds active.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing or cyanosis. Right index finger with mild periungual erythema (grade 1 paronychia), stable -- no fluctuance, no purulence.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Gait is normal. No ataxia. Speech is fluent and articulate. No cerebellar signs on finger-nose-finger testing.

Skin: As above -- residual grade 1 rash on face, improving.

---

LABORATORY DATA (drawn within 1 week of today's visit)

Complete Blood Count:
  WBC: 6.8 x10^9/L (normal)
  Hemoglobin: 12.1 g/dL (mild anemia, stable compared to prior)
  Platelets: 248 x10^9/L (normal)
  ANC: 4.2 x10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
  Creatinine: 0.74 mg/dL (eGFR >60, stable)
  BUN: 14 mg/dL
  Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
  ALT: 28 U/L (normal)
  AST: 22 U/L (normal)
  Alkaline phosphatase: 72 U/L (normal)
  Albumin: 3.7 g/dL
  Glucose: 94 mg/dL

LFTs: Within normal limits. No hepatotoxicity attributable to osi.
Electrolytes: Normal; specifically QTc-related electrolytes (K, Mg) within normal limits.

Magnesium: 2.1 mg/dL (normal).

ECG: QTc 428 ms (normal, no interval prolongation of concern).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (reviewed today, obtained prior to this visit):

Compared to pre-treatment baseline (initial staging PET-CT):

Chest: The right upper lobe mass has decreased substantially in size, now measuring 3.1 x 2.4 cm in its longest cross-sectional dimensions, down from 5.8 x 4.2 cm at diagnosis -- roughly a 47% reduction in the longest dimension. The mass appears less solid and more heterogeneous centrally, which may reflect areas of treatment-related necrosis or fibrosis. There is no significant pleural effusion. No endobronchial lesion identified.

Hilar and Mediastinal Nodes: Right hilar lymph nodes have decreased significantly and now measure approximately 0.9 cm in short axis (previously up to 1.9 cm). Right paratracheal (4R) station node is now 0.7 cm in short axis (previously approximately 1.4 cm on PET-CT). The previously hypermetabolic right supraclavicular node is no longer clearly measurable on this CT (consistent with the finding today that the node is no longer palpable on exam).

Abdomen and Pelvis: No hepatic lesions identified. No adrenal lesions. No abdominal or pelvic lymphadenopathy. No ascites.

Bones (partially imaged): No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions within the field of view.

Overall Impression: Excellent radiographic response to osi -- the primary right upper lobe tumor has shrunk by nearly half and the regional lymph nodes have decreased markedly across all previously involved stations. No new sites of disease have appeared in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. This is consistent with a meaningful partial response to treatment.

(Note: Brain MRI not obtained at this 3-month interval as per plan; will reassess CNS surveillance timing at 6-12 months per current evidence.)

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IIIB NSCLC, adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant (T3 N3 M0) -- Currently on Tagrisso 80 mg daily, cycle 3 (month 3)

Ms. Yuen has had an excellent early response to osi. Imaging today confirms a substantial partial response -- the right upper lobe primary has shrunk from 5.8 cm to 3.1 cm (longest dimension), and previously enlarged hilar, mediastinal, and supraclavicular nodes are now either below the threshold for pathologic enlargement or not visible at all. The clinical response correlates nicely with her symptom improvement: cough resolved, hemoptysis resolved, weight recovery in progress, and performance status improved to ECOG 0-1.

I reviewed these results with Ms. Yuen in detail today. She expressed great relief and was visibly encouraged by the findings. We discussed the significance of the tumor response and that Tagrisso works by blocking the EGFR pathway that is driving her cancer's growth.

Plan:
- Continue osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily without dose modification. No toxicity necessitating adjustment at this time.
- No concurrent chemotherapy or radiation planned at this time; definitive treatment remains Tagrisso monotherapy as established at tumor board.
- Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis planned in approximately 3 months.
- Will incorporate MRI brain at the 6-month or 12-month interval given EGFR-mutant disease and risk of CNS metastasis, even in the absence of current neurological symptoms. I will revisit timing at the next visit.

2. Acneiform rash (grade 1, improving) -- class-effect EGFR TKI toxicity
- Continue topical clindamycin 1% and urea-based moisturizer.
- Encouraged sun protection (SPF 30+, wide-brimmed hat) given photosensitivity risk with ongoing EGFR inhibition.
- Patient educated to contact clinic promptly if rash worsens to grade 2 or above (covering >30% BSA, significantly painful, requiring systemic antibiotics).
- No dose interruption warranted at current grade.

3. Grade 1 paronychia, right index finger
- Continue warm water soaks twice daily and topical antibiotic (mupirocin 2% ointment added today as topical clindamycin is being used for facial rash; using same product for both is reasonable but I prefer mupirocin for focal periungual infection).
- Patient advised against cutting cuticles, wearing well-fitting footwear, and avoiding trauma to nails.
- Referral to dermatology if worsening or development of additional paronychia affecting multiple digits.

4. Grade 1 diarrhea (ongoing but improved)
- Continue dietary modifications (low-fiber diet, BRAT-adjacent diet adjustments).
- Patient has loperamide on hand for use as needed; instructed to use if stools increase to 4+ above baseline per day.
- Adequate hydration discussed.

5. Mild anemia (Hgb 12.1 g/dL)
- Mild, likely multifactorial (chronic disease, prior nutritional depletion from weight loss). Stable on serial labs.
- No transfusion threshold met. Monitor at next visit. Iron studies may be considered if progressive.

6. Cancer status
- Cancer is present at this assessment but responding well to targeted therapy.
- Prognosis discussed -- EGFR-mutant NSCLC on Tagrisso has demonstrated median progression-free survival data in the range of 18-24 months in advanced-stage trials; in stage III disease (LAURA trial data and related evidence), duration of benefit may be prolonged. Patient understands this is not curative but that meaningful disease control is a realistic goal.
- Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and is in good spirits. She returns to the clinic in 3 months or sooner if new symptoms arise.

Follow-up: Approximately 3 months for repeat imaging and clinical assessment.
Emergency contact: On file. Patient given oncology on-call number for urgent issues.

---

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes, greater than 50% in discussion and counseling.

Electronically signed by: [Attending Oncologist, MD]
Department of Hematology/Oncology
